incoming/identify-three-dimensional-figu: A cube has 6 equal square faces, 8 vertices and 12 edges.
A cone has one circular base that narrows to a single point, called the apex.
A cylinder has two flat circular ends and one curved surface. A cone has only one circular end.
Sphere, cylinder and cone each have a curved surface. Cube, rectangular prism and square pyramid are made of flat faces only.
A cube has 6 square faces — just like the 6 sides of a dice.
A party hat is a cone — a circular base rising to one point.
A sphere is perfectly round everywhere — like a basketball or a globe.
A solid keeps its name no matter how tall, short or turned around it is. Two cylinders, two cubes and two cones.
A square pyramid: a square base with 4 triangular faces folding up to meet at one point.
Its faces are rectangles that are not all the same size, so it is a rectangular prism. A cube is the special rectangular prism whose faces are all equal squares.
Cube, rectangular prism and square pyramid are built from flat faces only. Sphere, cone and cylinder each have a curved surface.
A sphere has no flat face at all, so it just rolls away. Cubes, cylinders and rectangular prisms all have flat faces to stack on.
6 equal squares fold up into a cube — one square for each face.
The square is the base, and the 4 triangles fold up to meet at one point — a square pyramid.
Rectangle + 2 circles rolls into a cylinder. A fan + 1 circle curls into a cone. 6 rectangles fold into a rectangular prism. 6 equal squares fold into a cube.
Only the first net is made of 6 equal squares. The others contain triangles or circles, so they fold into other solids.
4 edges around the top + 4 around the bottom + 4 standing up = 12 edges.
4 corners on the top face + 4 corners on the bottom face = 8 vertices.
A sphere is smooth all over, so it has no corners. A cone still has 1 vertex — the point at the top.
4 triangular faces + 1 square base = 5 faces. It is easy to forget the base!
The top circle and the bottom circle are flat — that is 2 flat faces. The side is curved, so it does not count.
Sphere has 0 flat faces, cone has 1 (its base), cylinder has 2 (both ends), and a cube has 6.
Dice = cube. Beach ball and globe = spheres. Tin can and drum = cylinders. Party hat = cone.
The tin can and the drum are cylinders — a flat circle at each end. The hat is a cone, the dice is a cube and the ball is a sphere.
Front, back, left, right, top and bottom = 6 rectangular faces — the same number as a cube.

3 blue shapes out of 4 shapes in all = 3/4.
The first group has 1 green out of 2 shapes = 1/2.
The second group has 2 green out of 3 shapes = 2/3.
The third group has 3 green out of 5 shapes = 3/5.
The fourth group has 1 green out of 4 shapes = 1/4.
5 shapes in all; 2 are stars. That is 2/5.
4 shapes in all; 3 are hearts. That is 3/4.
3 out of 5 shapes, so 3/5.
3 out of 4 shapes, so 3/4.
4 out of 6 shapes, so 4/6.
1/2 means 1 of the 2 shapes goes in.
3/4 means 3 of the 4 shapes go in.
2/5 means 2 of the 5 shapes go in.
5/6 means 5 of the 6 shapes go in — almost all of them!
1/2 and 2/4 both mean half the group is green.
1/3 and 2/6 are the same amount — one third of the group.
When every shape is green, the fraction is the whole group: 2/2 and 3/3 both mean ALL.
Out of 4 shapes: 1 is less than 2 (a half), which is less than 3.
Same group of 6: 1 part < 3 parts < 5 parts.
The bottom number is the WHOLE — every shape in the group.
The top number is the PART — how many you are counting.
2 triangles out of 5 shapes = 2/5.
Same group! 3 circles out of 5 shapes = 3/5. The whole (5) stayed the same — only the part changed.
incoming/symmetry_remix.html:0: A rectangle has only 2 lines of symmetry — straight up-and-down and straight across. The diagonal doesn't fold to match. No.
incoming/symmetry_remix.html:1: The vertical line from the top point splits the two equal sides evenly. That's the one.
incoming/symmetry_remix.html:2: A square has 4 lines of symmetry: up-down, across, and both diagonals. All four are correct!
incoming/symmetry_remix.html:3: A rectangle has just 2 lines of symmetry — up-down and across. Its diagonals do NOT match up.
incoming/symmetry_remix.html:4: One line from each corner to the middle of the opposite side — that's 3 lines of symmetry.
incoming/symmetry_remix.html:5: A slanted parallelogram has NO lines of symmetry — no fold makes the two halves match. (It does have rotational symmetry, which is different!)
incoming/symmetry_remix.html:6: Heart → 1, isosceles triangle → 1. Square → 4 and circle → endless, so both go in "more than 1".
incoming/symmetry_remix.html:7: A folds down the middle (vertical). B folds across (horizontal). H folds BOTH ways, so it sits in the overlap. F folds neither way — it stays outside.
incoming/symmetry_remix.html:8: The line straight down the middle folds the square into two matching halves. (The two side midpoints work too!)
incoming/symmetry_remix.html:9: From the top point straight down to the middle of the base — that fold matches the two equal sides.
incoming/symmetry_remix.html:10: The centre is halfway across and halfway up — both lines of symmetry pass through it.
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:0: The line runs from the top point straight down to the opposite dip, splitting the star into matching halves. Yes.
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:1: The letter P has no line of symmetry — the bump is only on one side, so the halves don't match. No.
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:2: A kite has one line of symmetry — its long line. The two sides fold to match. Yes.
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:3: A scalene triangle (all sides different) has no lines of symmetry. No fold matches. No.
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:4: The vertical line from the roof tip straight down splits the house into matching halves.
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:5: The vertical line down the middle matches the two equal slanted sides. That's the one.
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:6: A regular pentagon has 5 equal sides, so it has 5 lines of symmetry — one from each corner.
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:7: A rectangle has exactly 2 lines of symmetry — up-and-down and across. Its diagonals don't match up.
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:8: Every line through the centre of a circle is a line of symmetry — there are endless numbers of them!
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:9: A regular octagon has 8 equal sides, so it has 8 lines of symmetry.
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:10: An equilateral triangle has 3 lines of symmetry — one from each corner to the middle of the opposite side. All three are correct!
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:11: Only up-and-down and across work. A rectangle's diagonal is a common trap — it does NOT fold to match.
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:12: Cards 1, 2, and 3 all pass through the centre and match the halves. Card 4 is off to the side, so it does NOT work.
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:13: A plus (Greek cross) has 4 lines of symmetry — up-down, across, and both diagonals. All four match!
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:14: Scalene triangle → 0. Isosceles triangle → 1. Square → 4 and pentagon → 5, so both go in "more than 1".
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:15: Circle → endless lines, letter A → one vertical line. Parallelogram and letter F have none — no fold matches.
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:16: T folds down the middle (vertical). D folds across (horizontal). O folds BOTH ways, so it sits in the overlap. R folds neither way — it stays outside.
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:17: A regular hexagon has 6 lines of symmetry: 3 that join opposite corners, and 3 that join the middles of opposite sides. You need all six!
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:18: The line of symmetry is the vertical line halfway between the dots — every point on it is the same distance from both dots.
incoming/symmetry_set2.html:19: The centre sits in the exact middle. Every line of symmetry passes through this one point.

incoming/time_units_remix.html:0: 4+1 = 5 hr, 10+18 = 28 min. No carry needed.
incoming/time_units_remix.html:3: 17 = 12 + 5, and 12 months = 1 year, so 17 months = 1 year 5 months.
incoming/time_units_remix.html:4: 12+15 = 27 hr = 1 day 3 hr. 1+3+1 = 5 days. → 5 days 3 hours.
incoming/time_units_remix.html:5: 5+4 = 9 days = 1 week 2 days. 3+2+1 = 6 weeks. → 6 weeks 2 days.

incoming/time_units_remix.html:7: Borrow 1 day (24 hr): 5 days 8 hours = 4 days 32 hours. Now 32−10 is easy.
incoming/time_units_remix.html:8: Borrow: 5 days 8 hours = 4 days 32 hours. 32−10 = 22 hr, days stay 4. → 4 days 22 hours.
incoming/time_units_remix.html:9: Borrow: 8 years = 7 years 12 months. 12−1 = 11 mo, 7−4 = 3 yr. → 3 years 11 months.
incoming/time_units_remix.html:10: Borrow: 3 weeks = 2 weeks 7 days. 7−5 = 2 days, 2−2 = 0 weeks. → 2 days.
incoming/time_units_remix.html:12: 53+9 = 62 s = 1 min 2 s. 3+3+1 = 7 min. → 7 minutes 2 seconds.
incoming/time_units_remix.html:13: Only 2 months < 8 months forces a borrow. In the others the top part is already bigger.
incoming/time_units_remix.html:14: 45+30 = 75 min = 1 hr 15 min. 2+1+1 = 4 hr. → 4 hours 15 minutes.
incoming/time_units_remix.html:15: 2 days 6 hours = 1 day 30 hours. 30−20 = 10 hr. → 1 day 10 hours.
incoming/time_units_variations.html:0: 7 days = 1 week, so 7 or more days regroup into weeks.
incoming/time_units_variations.html:1: 12 months = 1 year, so 12 or more months regroup into years.
incoming/time_units_variations.html:2: 6 weeks 1 day = 5 weeks 8 days. 8−4 = 4 days, 5−2 = 3 weeks. → 3 weeks 4 days.
incoming/time_units_variations.html:3: 7 years 2 months = 6 years 14 months. 14−8 = 6 months, 6−3 = 3 years. → 3 years 6 months.
incoming/time_units_variations.html:4: Borrowing 1 week gives 7 days, so 2 days becomes 2+7 = 9 days → 2 weeks 9 days.
incoming/time_units_variations.html:5: 15 months = 1 year 3 months, so 7 years 15 months = 8 years 3 months.
incoming/time_units_variations.html:6: 18+9 = 27 hr = 1 day 3 hr. 3+2+1 = 6 days. → 6 days 3 hours.
incoming/time_units_variations.html:7: Borrow: 5 weeks 3 days = 4 weeks 10 days. 10−6 = 4 days, 4−2 = 2 weeks. → 2 weeks 4 days.
incoming/time_units_variations.html:8: 9+6 = 15 mo = 1 year 3 months. 3+4+1 = 8 years. → 8 years 3 months.
incoming/time_units_variations.html:9: Days regroup at 7 (a week), months at 12 (a year), hours at 24 (a day).
incoming/time_units_variations.html:10: 27 hr ≥ 24, 15 mo ≥ 12, and 10 days ≥ 7 regroup. 6 days and 19 hr stay under their limits.
incoming/time_units_variations.html:11: 45+35 = 80 s = 1 min 20 s. 1+1+1 = 3 min. → 3 minutes 20 seconds.
incoming/time_units_variations.html:12: 3 days 4 hours = 2 days 28 hours. 28−9 = 19 hr, 2−1 = 1 day. → 1 day 19 hours.
incoming/time_units_variations.html:13: Borrow: 6 hours 15 minutes = 5 hours 75 minutes. 75−40 = 35 min, 5−2 = 3 hr. → 3 hours 35 minutes.
